Mojave Nature by Kurt Buzard


Eschscholzia glyptosperma

Desert Gold Poppy

Eschscholzia glyptosperma is often confused with E. parishii, but it can be identified by its strictly basal leaves and its seeds, which are distinctly pitted or carved (hence the name glyptosperma). It is a quintessential spring annual of the Mojave Desert. A brilliant yellow poppy of the desert flats, distinguished by its deeply pitted seeds and tufted growth habit.

Learn More Details About This Plant

Plant Data

Names and Taxonomy

  • Common Names: desert gold poppymojave poppy
  • Synonyms:
  • Family: papaveraceae
  • Genus Information: CA 12, NV 5, AZ 4, UT 3, NM 1

Habit and Size

  • Duration: annual herb
  • Habit: erect / scapose
  • Shape: tufted
  • Size: 5–25 cm tall
  • Stem: leafless flowering stalks (scapes) arising from a basal tuft

Leaves

  • Color: gray-green to glaucous
  • Shape: highly dissected / ternately branched
  • Edge: lobed
  • Arrangement: basal rosette
  • Leaf Retention: annual

Flowers and Fruit

  • Sex: hermaphroditic
  • Flower Color: bright yellow
  • Flower Size: 1.5–2.5 cm diameter
  • Number of Petals: 4
  • Bloom: March–May
  • Fruit: elongated capsule, 4–7 cm long

Habitat and Growing Conditions

  • Water Use: low
  • Light: full sun
  • Soil: sandy or gravelly washes and flats
  • Habitat: creosote bush scrub, desert washes

Distribution

  • Distribution: CA, NV, AZ, UT
  • Seen: Ubehebe Crater, Death Valley
  • Elevation: 0–4000 feet (0–1200 meters)
